html:
Act I

A very large, rather dirty room, with whitewashed walls. To the left, in a niche, is a window, the only outside window in the room, opening on a courtyard. The light from it is so dim that even by day the electricity has to be turned on.

At the very top of the centre-back wall is a row of small dusty windows. They open on the circus hall. At night, when the performance is going on, a bright light shines through. By day they are dark. In the same wall is a large white door, reached by two stone steps, and nailed fast.

On the right, almost in the corner, is a high, wide, arched doorway which leads to the stables and the ring. By day it opens into pale darkness, at night into pale light.

The room is used for many purposes. It is the office of Papa Briquet, manager of the circus; here he keeps his little desk. It is the cloakroom of some of the actors. It is also the room where the cast gathers between calls, during rehearsals or performances. Again, it is a checkroom for used circus property, such as gilt armchairs, scenery for pantomimes, and other wares of the circus household. The walls are covered with circus announcements and glaring posters.

The time is morning. In the circus hall a rehearsal is going on, and preparations are being made for the evening performance. As the curtain goes up, the cracking whip and the shouts of the riding-master are heard from the ring. The stage is empty for a few seconds, then enter Tilly and Polly, the musical clowns, practising a new march. Playing on tiny pipes, they step from the dark doorway to the window. Their music is agreeable to the ear, but small, mincing, artificially clown-like, like their mincing steps; they wear jackets and resemble each other; same smooth-shaven face, same height; Tilly, the younger, has a scarf around his neck; both have their derbies on the backs of their heads. Tilly glances through the window, then they turn about, still marching.

Polly Interrupting the march. Stop, you‚Äôre out again! Now, listen‚ÅÝ‚ÄîHe stands close to Tilly and plays into his face. Tilly absentmindedly listens, scratching his nose. There! Come on now! They resume their music and marching. As they reach the door they meet the manager and Mancini; the latter walks behind the manager, and is gnawing at the knob of his goldmounted cane. Count Mancini is tall and slight. The seams of his clothes are worn and he keeps his coat buttoned tight. He assumes extremely graceful manners, takes affected poses, and has a special fondness for toying with his cane, with aristocratic stylishness. When he laughs, which happens often, his thin sharp face takes on a marked resemblance to a satyr. The manager, ‚ÄúPapa‚Äù Briquet, is a stout quiet man of average height. His bearing is hesitant. The clowns make room for the gentlemen. The manager looks questioningly at the older man.
Polly With an affected accent. Our moosic for the pantomime! The March of the Ants!
Briquet Ha! Yes!
The gentlemen walk in. The clowns resume their music, Polly marching on, then turning, the younger following.
Polly Papa Briquet, Jack is working very badly today.
Briquet What‚Äôs the matter with him?
Polly He has a sore throat. You‚Äôd better take a look at him.
Briquet All right. Come on, Jack. Open your mouth! Wider‚ÅÝ‚Äîwider. Turns clown‚Äôs face to the light near the window and examines him closely and seriously. Just smear it with iodine.
Polly I told him so. I said it was nothing! Oh! Come on. They go away playing, marching, practising their funny mincing steps. The manager sits down. Mancini strikes a pose by the wall, smiling ironically.
Mancini So. You give them medical treatment, too! Look out, Papa Briquet, you have no licence.
Briquet Just a little advice. They‚Äôre all so afraid for their lives.
Mancini His throat is simply burnt with whiskey. These two fellows get drunk every night. I am amazed, Papa Briquet, to see you pay so little attention to their morals. He laughs.
Briquet You make me sick, Mancini.
Mancini Count Mancini is at your service!
Briquet You make me sick, Count Mancini. You poke your nose into everything, you disturb the artists in their work. Some day you‚Äôll get a thrashing, and I warn you that I shan‚Äôt interfere.
Mancini As a man of superior associations and education I cannot be expected to treat your actors as my equals! What more can you ask, Briquet? You see that I do you the honour of speaking with you quite familiarly, quite simply.
Briquet Ha! ha! ha! Slightly threatening. Really!‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Mancini Never mind my joke. What if they did dare attack me‚ÅÝ‚Äîever seen this, Briquet? He draws a stiletto out of his cane and advances it silently. Useful little thing. By the way, you have no idea of the discovery I made yesterday in a suburb. Such a girl! Laughs. Oh, well! all right, all right‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI know you don‚Äôt like that sort of sport. But look here, you must give me a hundred francs!
Briquet Not a sou.
Mancini Then I‚Äôll take away Consuelo‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat‚Äôs all‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Briquet Your daily threat!
Mancini Yes, my threat! And you would do the same, if you were as shamefully hard up as I am. Now look here, you know as well as I do that I have to live up to my name somehow, keep up the family reputation. Just because the tide of ill-fortune which struck my ancestors compelled me to make my daughter, the Countess Veronica, a bareback rider‚ÅÝ‚Äîto keep us from starving‚ÅÝ‚Äîdo you understand‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou heartless idiot!
Briquet You chase the girls too much! Some day you‚Äôll land in jail, Mancini!
Mancini In jail? Oh, no! Why, I have to uphold our name, the splendour of my family, laughs haven‚Äôt I? The Mancinis are known all over Italy for their love of girls‚ÅÝ‚Äîjust girls! Is it my fault if I must pay such crazy prices for what my ancestors got free of charge? You‚Äôre nothing but an ass, a parvenu ass. How can you understand Family Traditions? I don‚Äôt drink‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI stopped playing cards after that accident‚ÅÝ‚Äîno, you need not smile. Now if I give up the girls, what will be left of Mancini? Only a coat of arms, that‚Äôs all‚ÅÝ‚ÄîIn the name of family traditions, give me a hundred francs!
Briquet I told you no, I won‚Äôt.
Mancini You know that I leave half of the salary for Consuelo‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut‚ÅÝ‚Äîperhaps you think I do not love my child‚ÅÝ‚Äîmy only daughter, all that remains to me as a memory of her sainted mother‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhat cruelty! Pretends to cry, wipes his eyes with a small and dirty lace handkerchief, embroidered with a coronet.
Briquet Why don‚Äôt you say, rather, that she is foolish enough to give you half her salary. You make me sick‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Enter Zinida, the lion tamer; burningly beautiful, her self-confident, commanding gestures at first glance give an impression of languor. She is Briquet‚Äôs unmarried wife.
Zinida To Mancini. Good morning.
Mancini Madame Zinida! This barbarian, this brute may pierce me with his dagger, but I cannot control the expression of my love! Kneels facetiously before her. Madame! Count Mancini has the honour of asking you to be his wife.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶
Zinida To Briquet. Money?
Briquet Yes.
Zinida Don‚Äôt give him any. Sits down wearily on a torn sofa, shuts her eyes. Mancini gets up and wipes his knees.
Mancini Duchess! Don‚Äôt be cruel. I am no lion, no tiger, no savage beast which you are accustomed to tame. I am merely a poor domestic animal, who wants, meow, meow, a little green grass.
Zinida Without opening her eyes. Jim tells me you have a teacher for Consuelo. What for?
Mancini The solicitude of a father, duchess, the solicitude and the tireless anxiety of a loving heart. The extreme misfortunes of our family, when I was a child, have left some flaws in her education. Friends, the daughter of Count Mancini, Countess Veronica, can barely read! Is that admissible? And you, Briquet, heartless brute, you still ask why I need money!
Zinida Artful!
Briquet What are you teaching her?
Mancini Everything. A student had been giving her lessons, but I threw him out yesterday. He had the nerve to fall in love with Consuelo and stood there meowing at the door like a cat. Everything, Briquet, that you don‚Äôt know‚ÅÝ‚Äîliterature, mythology, orthography‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Two young actresses appear, with small fur coats thrown over their light dresses. They are tired and sit down in the corner.
Mancini I do not wish my daughter‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Zinida Artful!
Briquet You are stupid, Mancini. What do you do it for? In a didactic tone. You are fearfully stupid, Mancini. Why does she need to learn? Since she is here she need never know anything about that life. Don‚Äôt you understand? What is geography? If I were the government I would forbid artists to read books. Let them read the posters, that‚Äôs enough.
During Briquet‚Äôs speech, the two clowns and another actor enter. They sit down wearily.
Briquet Right now, your Consuelo is an excellent artist, but just as soon as you teach her mythology, and she begins to read, she‚Äôll become a nuisance, she‚Äôll be corrupted, and then she‚Äôll go and poison herself. I know those books, I‚Äôve read ‚Äôem myself. All they teach is corruption, and how to kill oneself.
First Actress I love the novels that come out in the newspaper.
Briquet That shows what a foolish girl you are. You‚Äôll be done for in no time. Believe me, my friends, we must forget entirely what is happening out there. How can we understand all that goes on there?
Mancini You are an enemy of enlightenment, you are an obscurantist, Briquet.
Briquet And you are stupid. You are from out there. What has it taught you? The actors laugh. If you‚Äôd been born in a circus as I was, you‚Äôd know something. Enlightenment is plain nonsense‚ÅÝ‚Äînothing else. Ask Zinida. She knows everything they teach out there‚ÅÝ‚Äîgeography, mythology‚ÅÝ‚ÄîDoes it make her any happier? You tell them, dear.
Zinida Leave me alone, Louis.
Mancini Angrily. Oh! Go to the devil! When I listen to your asinine philosophy, I‚Äôd like to skin you for more than a paltry hundred francs‚ÅÝ‚Äîfor two hundred‚ÅÝ‚Äîfor a thousand. Great God! What an ass of a manager! Yes, right before every one of them I want to say that you are a stingy old skinflint‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat you pay starvation wages. I‚Äôll make you give Consuelo a raise of a hundred francs. Listen, all you honest vagabonds, tell me‚ÅÝ‚Äîwho is it draws the crowd that fills the circus every night? You? a couple of musical donkeys? Tigers, lions? Nobody cares for those hungry cats!
Zinida Leave the tigers alone.
Mancini Beg your pardon, Zinida. I did not mean to hurt your feelings‚ÅÝ‚Äîhonestly. I really marvel at your furious audacity‚ÅÝ‚Äîat your grace‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou are a heroine‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI kiss your tiny hands. But what do they understand about heroism? An orchestra softly plays the Tango in the circus. He continues with enthusiasm. Hear! hear! Now tell me, honest vagabonds, who but Consuelo and Bezano draws the crowds! That Tango on horseback‚ÅÝ‚Äîit is‚ÅÝ‚Äîit is‚ÅÝ‚ÄîOh, the devil! Even his fatuousness the Pope could not withstand its lure.
Polly True! It‚Äôs a great trick‚ÅÝ‚Äîwasn‚Äôt the idea Bezano‚Äôs?
Mancini Idea! Idea! The lad‚Äôs in love, like a cat‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat‚Äôs the idea. What‚Äôs the good of an idea without a woman! You wouldn‚Äôt dance very far with your idea alone, eh, Papa Briquet?
Briquet We have a contract.
Mancini Such base formalities.
Zinida Give him ten francs and let him go.
Mancini Ten! Never! Fifteen! Don‚Äôt be stubborn, Papa. For the traditions of my house‚ÅÝ‚Äîtwenty. I swear‚ÅÝ‚Äîon my honour‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI can‚Äôt do with less. Briquet hands him twenty francs. Nonchalantly. Merci. Thanks.
Zinida Why don‚Äôt you take it from your baron?
Mancini Raising his eyebrows haughtily, quite indignant. From the Baron? Woman! who do you think I am that I should be beholden to a stranger?
Zinida You‚Äôre plotting something artful. I know you very little, but I guess you‚Äôre an awful scoundrel.
Mancini Laughs. Such an insult from such beautiful lips.
Enter an ‚Äúartist,‚Äù apparently an athlete.
Athlete Papa Briquet, there‚Äôs a gentleman from beyond the grave asking for you.
Actress A ghost?
Athlete No. He seems alive. Did you ever see a drunken ghost?
Briquet If he‚Äôs drunk, tell him I‚Äôm out, Thomas. Does he want to see me or the Count?
Athlete No, you. Maybe he‚Äôs not drunk, but just a ghost.
Mancini Draws himself together, puffs up. A society man?
Athlete Yes. I‚Äôll tell him to come in.
One hears the whip cracking in the ring. The Tango sounds very low and distant‚ÅÝ‚Äîthen comes nearer‚ÅÝ‚Äîlouder. Silence.
Briquet Touching Zinida‚Äôs arm. Tired?
Zinida Drawing back a little. No.
Polly Your red lion is nervous today, Zinida!
Zinida You shouldn‚Äôt tease him.
Polly I played a melody from Traviata for him. And he sang with me. Wouldn‚Äôt that be a good trick to stage, Papa Briquet?
Thomas brings in the gentleman, points out the manager, and goes heavily away. The gentleman is not young, and he is ugly, but his rather strange face is bold and lively. He wears an expensive overcoat, with a fur collar, and holds his hat and gloves in his hand.
Gentleman Bowing and smiling. Have I the pleasure of addressing the manager?
Briquet Yes. Won‚Äôt you sit down, please? Tilly, bring a chair.
Gentleman Oh! Don‚Äôt trouble. Looks around. These are your artists? Very glad‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Mancini Straightening and bowing slightly. Count Mancini.
Gentleman Surprised. Count?
Briquet Indignantly. Yes, Count. And whom have I the honour of‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Gentleman I don‚Äôt quite know myself‚ÅÝ‚Äîyet. As a rule you choose your own names, don‚Äôt you? I have not chosen yet. Later you might advise me about it. I have an idea already, but I am afraid it sounds too much like literature‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou know.
Briquet Literature?
Gentleman Yes! Too sophisticated. They all look surprised. I presume these two gentlemen are clowns? I am so glad. May I shake hands with them? Stands up and shakes hands with clowns, who make silly faces.
Briquet Excuse me‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut what can I do for you?
Gentleman With the same pleasant, confident smile. Oh. You do something for me? No. I want to do something for you, Papa Briquet.
Briquet Papa Briquet? But you don‚Äôt look like‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Gentleman Reassuringly. It‚Äôs all right. I shall become ‚Äúlike.‚Äù These two gentlemen just made remarkable faces. Would you like to see me imitate them? Look! He makes the same silly faces as the clowns.
Briquet Yes! Involuntarily. You are not drunk, sir?
Gentleman No. I don‚Äôt drink as a rule. Do I look drunk?
Polly A little.
Gentleman No‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI don‚Äôt drink. It is a peculiarity of my talent.
Briquet Familiarly. Where did you work before? Juggler?
Gentleman No. But I am glad you feel in me a comrade, Papa Briquet. Unfortunately I am not a juggler, and have worked nowhere‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI am‚ÅÝ‚Äîjust so.
Mancini But you look like a society man.
Gentleman Oh, you flatter me, Count. I am just so.
Briquet Well, what do you want? You see I am obliged to tell you that everything is taken.
Gentleman That‚Äôs immaterial. I want to be a clown, if you will allow me. Some of the actors smile, Briquet begins to grow angry.
Briquet But what can you do? You‚Äôre asking too much. What can you do?
Gentleman Why! Nothing! Isn‚Äôt that funny! I can‚Äôt do a thing.
Briquet No, it‚Äôs not funny. Any scoundrel knows that much.
Gentleman Rather helpless, but still smiling and looking around. We can invent something‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Briquet Ironically. From literature?
The clown Jackson enters slowly without being noticed by the others. He stands behind the gentlemen.
Gentleman Yes, one can find something literary, too. A nice little speech for instance on, let‚Äôs say, a religious topic. Something like a debate among the clowns.
Briquet A debate! The devil! This is no academy.
Gentleman Sadly. I am very sorry. Something else then. Perhaps a joke about the creation of the world and its rulers?
Briquet What about the police? No, no‚ÅÝ‚Äînothing like that!
Jackson Coming forward. The rulers of the world? You don‚Äôt like them? I don‚Äôt either. Shake.
Briquet Introducing. Our chief clown, the famous Jackson.
Gentleman Enthusiastically. Great heavens‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou! Allow me to shake hands with you heartily! You, with your genius, you have given me so much joy!
Jackson I‚Äôm glad indeed!
Briquet Shrugs his shoulders; to Jackson. He wants to be a clown! Look him over, Jim.
Jackson makes a motion at which the gentleman hurriedly removes his coat and throws it on a chair. He is ready for the examination. Jackson turns him round, looking him over critically.
Jackson Clown? Hm! Turn round then. Clown? Yes? Now smile. Wider‚ÅÝ‚Äîbroader‚ÅÝ‚Äîdo you call that a smile? So‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat‚Äôs better. There is something, yes‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut for full developments‚ÅÝ‚ÄîSadly. Probably you can‚Äôt even turn a somersault?
Gentleman Sighs. No.
Jackson How old are you?
Gentleman Thirty-nine. Too late? Jackson moves away with a whistle. There is a silence.
Zinida Softly. Take him.
Briquet Indignant. What the hell shall I do with him if he doesn‚Äôt know a thing? He‚Äôs drunk!
Gentleman Honestly I am not. Thank you for your support, Madame. Are you not the famous Zinida, the lion tamer, whose regal beauty and audacity‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Zinida Yes. But I do not like flattery.
Gentleman It is not flattery.
Mancini You are evidently not accustomed to good society, my dear. Flattery? This gentleman expresses his admiration in sincere and beautiful words‚ÅÝ‚Äîand you‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou are not educated, Zinida. As for myself‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Enter Consuelo and Bezano in circus costume.
Consuelo You here, Daddy?
Mancini Yes, my child, you are not tired? Kisses her on the forehead. My daughter, sir, Countess Veronica. Known on the stage as Consuelo, The Bareback Tango Queen. Did you ever see her?
Gentleman I have enjoyed her work. It is marvellous!
Mancini Yes! Of course. Everyone admits it. And how do you like the name, Consuelo? I took it from the novel of George Sand. It means ‚ÄúConsolation.‚Äù
Gentleman What a wonderful knowledge of books!
Mancini A small thing. Despite your strange intention, I can see, sir, that you are a gentleman. My peer! Let me explain to you, that only the strange and fatal misfortunes of our ancient family‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äúsic transit gloria mundi,‚Äù sir.
Consuelo It‚Äôs a bore, Daddy‚ÅÝ‚ÄîWhere‚Äôs my handkerchief, Alfred?
Bezano Here it is.
Consuelo Showing the handkerchief to the gentleman. Genuine Venetian. Do you like it?
Gentleman Again bowing. My eyes are dazzled, how beautiful! Papa Briquet, the more I look around me the more I want to stay with you. Makes the face of a simpleton. On the one hand a count, on the other‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Jackson Nods approval. That‚Äôs not bad. Look here, think a bit‚ÅÝ‚Äîfind something. Everyone here thinks for himself.
Silence. The gentleman stands with a finger on his forehead, thinking.
Gentleman Find something‚ÅÝ‚Äîfind something‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Eureka!
Polly That means found. Come!
Gentleman Eureka‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI shall be among you, he who gets slapped. General laughter. Even Briquet smiles.
Gentleman Looks at them smiling. You see I made even you laugh‚ÅÝ‚Äîis that easy? All grow serious. Polly sighs.
Tilly No, it‚Äôs not easy. Did you laugh, Polly?
Polly Sure, a lot. Did you?
Tilly I did. Imitating an instrument, he plays with his lips a melody at once sad and gay.
Jackson ‚ÄúHe Who Gets Slapped,‚Äù that‚Äôs not bad.
Gentleman It‚Äôs not, is it? I rather like it myself. It suits my talent. And comrades, I have even found a name‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou‚Äôll call me ‚ÄúHe.‚Äù Is that all right?
Jackson Thinking. ‚ÄúHe‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚ÄîNot bad.
Consuelo In a singing, melodic voice. ‚ÄúHe‚Äù is so funny‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄúHe‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Äîlike a dog. Daddy, are there such dogs?
Jackson suddenly gives a circus slap to the gentleman. He steps back and grows pale.
Gentleman What!‚ÅÝ‚ÄîGeneral laughter covers his exclamation.
Jackson He Who Gets Slapped. Or didn‚Äôt you get it?
Polly Comically. He says he wants more‚ÅÝ‚ÄîThe gentleman smiles, rubbing his cheek.
Gentleman So sudden.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîWithout waiting.‚ÅÝ‚ÄîHow funny‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou didn‚Äôt hurt me, and yet my cheek burns.
Again there is loud laughter. The clowns cackle like ducks, hens, cocks; they bark. Zinida says something to Briquet, casts a glance toward Bezano, and goes out. Mancini assumes a bored air and looks at his watch. The two actresses go out.
Jackson Take him, Papa Briquet‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe will push us.
Mancini Again looking at his watch. But bear in mind, that Papa Briquet is as close as Harpagon. If you expect to get good money here you are mistaken. He laughs. A slap? What‚Äôs a slap? Worth only small change, a franc and a half a dozen. Better go back to society; you will make more money there. Why for one slap, just a light tap, you might say, my friend, Marquis Justi, was paid fifty thousand lire!
Briquet Shut up, Mancini. Will you take care of him, Jackson.
Jackson I can.
Polly Do you like music? A Beethoven sonata played on a broom, for instance, or Mozart on a bottle?
He Alas! No. But I will be exceedingly grateful if you will teach me. A clown! My childhood‚Äôs dream. When all my school friends were thrilled by Plutarch‚Äôs heroes, or the light of science‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI dreamed of clowns. Beethoven on a broom, Mozart on bottles! Just what I have sought all my life! Friends, I must have a costume!
Jackson I see you don‚Äôt know much! A costume putting his finger on his forehead is a thing which calls for deep thought. Have you seen my Sun here? Strikes his posterior. I looked for it two years.
He Enthusiastically. I shall think!
Mancini It is time for me to go. Consuelo, my child, you must get dressed. To He. We are lunching with Baron Regnard, a friend of mine, a banker.
Consuelo But I don‚Äôt want to go, Daddy. Alfred says I must rehearse today.
Mancini Horrified, holding up his hands. Child, think of me, and what a situation you put me in! I promised the Baron, the Baron expects us. Why, it is impossible! Oh, I am in a cold sweat.
Consuelo Alfred says‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Bezano Drily. She has to work. Are you rested? Then come on.
Mancini But‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe devil take me if I know what to make of it. Hey, Bezano, bareback rider! Are you crazy? I gave you permission for Art‚Äôs sake, to exercise my daughter‚Äôs talent‚ÅÝ‚Äîand you‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Consuelo Go along, Papa, and don‚Äôt be so silly. We‚Äôve got to work, haven‚Äôt we? Have lunch along with your Baron. And Daddy, you forgot to take a clean handkerchief again, and I washed two for you yesterday. Where did you put them?
Mancini Ashamed, blushing. Why, my linen is washed by the laundress, and you, Consuelo, are still playing with toys. It is stupid! You‚Äôre a chatterbox. You don‚Äôt think. These gentlemen might imagine Heaven knows what. How stupid. I‚Äôm off.
Consuelo Do you want me to write him a little note?
Mancini Angrily. A little note? Your little notes would make a horse laugh! Goodbye.
He goes out toying angrily with his cane. The clowns follow him respectfully, playing a funeral march. He and Jackson laugh. The actors disappear one by one.
Consuelo Laughing. Do I really write so badly? And I love so to write. Did you like my note, Alfred‚ÅÝ‚Äîor did you laugh, too?
Bezano Blushing. No, I did not. Come on, Consuelo.
They go, and meet Zinida, entering. Consuelo passes on.
Zinida Are you going back to work, Bezano?
Bezano Politely. Yes. Today is a very bad day. How are your lions, Zinida? I think the weather affects them.
Consuelo From the ring. Alfred!
Zinida Yes. Someone is calling you. You‚Äôd better go. Alfred goes out. To Briquet. Are you finished?
Briquet Right away.
Jackson Then goodbye till evening. Think about your costume, He, and I shall look for some idea, too. Be here at ten tomorrow. Don‚Äôt be late, or you‚Äôll get another slap. And I‚Äôll work with you.
He I shall not be late. He looks after Jackson who goes out. Must be a nice man. All the people about you are so nice, Papa Briquet. I suppose that good-looking bareback rider is in love with Consuelo, isn‚Äôt he? Laughs.
Zinida It‚Äôs none of your business. For a newcomer you go poking your nose too far. How much does he want, Papa?
Briquet Just a minute. See here He. I don‚Äôt want to make a contract with you.
He Just as you please. Do you know what? Don‚Äôt let us talk about money. You are an honest fellow, Briquet; you will see what my work is worth to you, and then‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Briquet Pleased. Now that‚Äôs very nice of you. Zinida, the man really doesn‚Äôt know anything.
Zinida Well, do as he suggests. Now we must write it down. Where‚Äôs the book?
Briquet Here. To He. I don‚Äôt like to write gives book to Zinida, but we have to put down the names of the actors, you know‚ÅÝ‚Äîit‚Äôs police regulations. Then if anyone kills himself, or‚ÅÝ‚Äî
Again comes the sound of the Tango, and calls from the ring.
Zinida What is your name?
He Smiling. He. I chose it, you know. Or don‚Äôt you like it?
Briquet We like it all right‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut we have to have your real name. Have you a passport?
He Confused. A passport? No, I have none. Or, rather, yes. I have something of the kind, but I had no idea the rules were strictly enforced here. What do you need papers for?
Zinida and Briquet look at each other. Zinida pushes the book aside.
Zinida Then we can‚Äôt take you. We cannot quarrel with the police, just on your account.
Briquet She is my wife. I hadn‚Äôt told you. She‚Äôs right. You might get hurt by a horse, or hurt yourself‚ÅÝ‚Äîor do something. We don‚Äôt know you, you see.
